questions.q: The State Emblem of India is adapted from which ancient sculpture?
questions.explanation: The National Emblem is adapted from the Lion Capital of Ashoka.

questions.q: At which site was the original Lion Capital of Ashoka erected?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital was originally erected at Sarnath, near Varanasi.

questions.q: What is the motto inscribed below the National Emblem?
questions.explanation: The motto inscribed below the emblem is 'Satyameva Jayate'.

questions.q: What does the motto 'Satyameva Jayate' mean in English?
questions.explanation: 'Satyameva Jayate' means 'Truth Alone Triumphs'.

questions.q: On which date was the State Emblem of India adopted?
questions.explanation: The State Emblem of India was adopted on 26 January 1950.

questions.q: In which script is the motto 'Satyameva Jayate' written on the emblem?
questions.explanation: The motto is inscribed in Devanagari script.

questions.q: How many lions are visible in the National Emblem as commonly depicted?
questions.explanation: Three lions are visible in the emblem, the fourth being hidden from view.

questions.q: The ruler associated with the original Lion Capital was which Mauryan emperor?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital was set up by the Mauryan emperor Ashoka.

questions.q: A wheel appears in the abacus of the National Emblem. What is this wheel called?
questions.explanation: The wheel in the emblem is the Ashoka Chakra, also called the Dharma Chakra (wheel of law).

questions.q: The National Emblem is used by which entity?
questions.explanation: The State Emblem is the official emblem of the Government of India.

questions.q: The original Lion Capital of Ashoka is preserved in which museum?
questions.explanation: The original Lion Capital is preserved in the Sarnath Museum.

questions.q: How many lions does the original Lion Capital of Ashoka actually have, standing back to back?
questions.explanation: The original Lion Capital has four lions standing back to back, only three of which are visible in the emblem's frontal view.

questions.q: The animals depicted on the abacus of the emblem, between the wheels, include the bull, the horse, the elephant and which other animal?
questions.explanation: The abacus carries sculptures of a lion, an elephant, a horse and a bull, separated by wheels.

questions.q: The motto 'Satyameva Jayate' is derived from which ancient Indian text?
questions.explanation: 'Satyameva Jayate' is taken from the Mundaka Upanishad.

questions.q: In the emblem, the lions and the abacus rest upon which feature?
questions.explanation: In the original capital, the abacus rests on a bell-shaped lotus, though this is omitted in the official emblem.

questions.q: The Ashoka Chakra from the emblem's abacus also appears at the centre of which national symbol?
questions.explanation: The Ashoka Chakra appears in navy blue at the centre of the national flag of India.

questions.q: The use of the State Emblem is regulated by a law enacted in which year?
questions.explanation: The State Emblem of India (Prohibition of Improper Use) Act was enacted in 2005.

questions.q: In the official two-dimensional representation of the emblem, how many lions are shown facing the viewer?
questions.explanation: Three lions are shown in the frontal representation, with the fourth hidden behind.

questions.q: The National Emblem appears on which of the following official items?
questions.explanation: The State Emblem appears on official items such as currency notes, passports and government documents.

questions.q: The wheels (chakras) on the abacus between the animals number how many in the original capital?
questions.explanation: The abacus of the original capital has four wheels alternating with the four animals.

questions.q: The motto 'Satyameva Jayate' is rendered in which language?
questions.explanation: The motto 'Satyameva Jayate' is in Sanskrit, written in the Devanagari script.

questions.q: The Lion Capital of Ashoka was originally surmounted by which feature that is not part of the official State Emblem?
options: A large wheel (Dharma Chakra) above the lions
questions.explanation: The original capital was crowned by a large Dharma Chakra above the lions, which has since been lost and is not in the emblem.

questions.q: The original Lion Capital was carved during which historical period?
options: The Mauryan period, around the 3rd century BCE
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital dates to the Mauryan period, around the 3rd century BCE, under Ashoka.

questions.q: In the official emblem, the words 'Satyameva Jayate' are placed where in relation to the abacus?
options: Below the abacus, on the supporting platform
questions.explanation: The motto is inscribed below the abacus on the platform supporting the emblem.

questions.q: The Lion Capital was erected by Ashoka to commemorate which event associated with Sarnath?
questions.explanation: Sarnath is where the Buddha delivered his first sermon, and the pillar marked this sacred site.

questions.q: The original Lion Capital is made primarily of which material?
questions.explanation: The Lion Capital is carved from a single block of polished Chunar sandstone.

questions.q: Which body formally approved the design of the State Emblem before its adoption in 1950?
options: The Government of India under the new Constitution
questions.explanation: The emblem was adopted by the Government of India as the official state emblem on 26 January 1950.

questions.q: The four animals on the abacus are often interpreted as guardians of the four directions; which of these is NOT among them?
questions.explanation: The four animals are the lion, elephant, horse and bull; the peacock is not among them.

questions.q: The Dharma Chakra depicted on the abacus is shown with how many spokes, matching the wheel on the national flag?
questions.explanation: The Ashoka Chakra has twenty-four spokes, the same number as on the national flag.

questions.q: Improper or unauthorised use of the State Emblem is prohibited chiefly to protect which characteristic of the emblem?
options: Its status as an official symbol of authority
questions.explanation: The 2005 Act restricts use of the emblem to preserve its dignity as an official symbol of state authority.
